next_steps: Send guided selling overview, bundle capabilities, and validation rules documentation
Send pricing information for enterprise license plan
Follow up periodically — prospect exploring internal HubSpot build first
Re-engage if internal build fails to meet requirements
call_meta.notes: Prospect was transparent about evaluation state. Exploring whether HubSpot native quoting can be configured to meet needs before committing budget. Internal champion asked to survey market options and pricing in parallel.

jtbd.job: Implement a guided quoting process that validates technical requirements before quote submission
jtbd.context: Sales reps build quotes in HubSpot but select SKUs without structured validation. Complex products in telecom require qualifying questions (e.g., integration method: flat file vs. external API) that determine whether additional resources like sales engineers need to be involved.
jtbd.desired_outcome: A question-driven quoting flow that gates progress based on answers, routes complex configurations to sales engineering, and ensures correct SKUs are on every order before it reaches approval

jtbd.job: Reduce post-quote rework by catching configuration issues at the point of sale
jtbd.context: Currently, configuration issues are caught at the approval stage or during implementation. The VP of Solutions is the approval bottleneck, asking questions that should have been resolved earlier in the process.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Shift validation upstream so implementation receives clean, technically validated orders with correct SKUs and no invalid build assumptions

jtbd.job: Improve customer satisfaction and reduce churn by aligning what is sold with what is delivered
jtbd.context: When quoting gaps are caught late, customers experience surprise scope changes during implementation. This erodes trust and contributes to churn.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Customers understand exactly what they are buying during the sales process, implementations run faster, and billing starts sooner

pains.pain: Approval bottleneck where key qualifying questions are asked too late in the quoting process
pains.trigger: Every quote comes to the VP of Solutions for approval. At that point, he discovers missing information and asks questions that should have been captured upfront by the sales rep.
pains.cost: Delayed quote turnaround. Sales cycle lengthens because quotes bounce back for rework. VP of Solutions time consumed by administrative review instead of strategic work.
pains.urgency_reason: This is the primary driver for the evaluation. Every quote cycle surfaces this problem.

pains.pain: Incorrect SKUs and invalid build assumptions on quotes
pains.trigger: Reps select SKUs without structured guidance. Complex telecom products have interdependencies (e.g., integration method determines whether a sales engineer must be involved) that reps may not understand or remember.
pains.cost: Implementation receives orders with wrong SKUs, causing rework, delayed time-to-billing, and customer dissatisfaction. Contributes to churn.
pains.urgency_reason: Directly impacts revenue recognition speed and customer retention

pains.pain: No guided selling or conditional logic in HubSpot native quoting
pains.trigger: Team tried to build structured quoting within HubSpot but cannot create question-driven flows, conditional gating, or auto-population of line items based on answers.
pains.cost: Sales reps operate without guardrails. Every quote is a blank canvas requiring institutional knowledge to build correctly.
pains.urgency_reason: Team is actively exploring whether HubSpot can be configured to meet this need before purchasing a tool

pains.pain: HubSpot catalog lacks bundle and sub-SKU support
pains.trigger: Products are bundled but HubSpot only supports flat SKU structures. No way to represent a bundle header with included component SKUs underneath.
pains.cost: Quotes cannot accurately represent the product structure. Bundles are represented as single SKUs without visibility into components, making it harder for customers and implementation teams to understand what was sold.
pains.urgency_reason: Prospect specifically called out bundle/sub-SKU support as a 'big plus' over HubSpot

pains.pain: No validation rules to prevent incompatible SKU combinations
pains.trigger: Certain products and configurations should not be quoted together, but HubSpot has no mechanism to block invalid combinations.
pains.cost: Invalid quotes reach approval or even reach the customer, requiring rework and eroding confidence in the sales process.
pains.urgency_reason: Mentioned as a valuable capability but not the primary driver for the evaluation

workflow_reality.current_process: Sales reps build quotes directly in HubSpot using a product catalog with SKUs defined by product files. Reps must know which SKUs to add based on customer needs. Completed quotes route to the VP of Solutions for approval. At the approval stage, qualifying questions are asked that should have been captured earlier. No structured decision tree or conditional logic exists in the quoting flow.
tools_in_use: HubSpot CRM (Sales Hub — quoting, deal management, product catalog)
workflow_reality.team_size_quoting: Multiple sales reps plus VP of Solutions as sole approver
brittleness_indicators: VP of Solutions is single approval bottleneck for all quotes
Institutional knowledge required to build correct quotes — no system-enforced guardrails
Configuration issues surface at approval or implementation, not at point of sale
Flat SKU structure cannot represent product bundling reality
No conditional logic or gating to route complex configurations to sales engineering

evaluated.name: HubSpot native quoting (internal build)
evaluated.what_they_liked: Already licensed. No additional cost. Team is familiar with the platform.
evaluated.why_it_may_not_work: HubSpot lacks guided selling, conditional logic, bundle/sub-SKU support, and validation rules. Prospect acknowledged 'there are some limitations in HubSpot' but wants to exhaust internal options first.
evaluated.key_quote: We're gonna see if we can't build something ourselves using HubSpot as it exists today.

evaluated.name: Other guided quoting vendors (unspecified)
evaluated.why_quotivity_compared_favorably: Prospect noted that other vendors he spoke with did not offer the same capabilities as Quotivity, particularly around guided selling and bundle support.
evaluated.key_quote: There are some things that you guys can do that, you know, if I talk to somebody else, they weren't the same.

current_workaround.description: HubSpot native quoting with manual SKU selection by reps and single-point approval by VP of Solutions who catches errors and asks qualifying questions at the approval stage
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional but error-prone. Validation happens too late. Quote quality depends on rep knowledge rather than system enforcement.

quotes.quote: Sometimes it's at that point that I'm asking these key questions. And we wanna capture that earlier in the process, not at the point that they're ready to send out a quote.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the core pain of approval-stage rework. Strong for content about moving validation upstream in the quoting process.

quotes.quote: We wanna be able to improve the upfront quoting process to ensure that we've got good quotes going out. They're accurate and consistent, and they've been technically validated up front.
quotes.usage_notes: Clean summary of desired outcome. Useful for positioning content around quote quality and technical validation.

quotes.quote: We don't wanna have incorrect SKUs and invalid build assumptions. Just make it easier as it goes down the process.
quotes.usage_notes: Directly ties quoting accuracy to downstream implementation quality. Strong for content about the cost of bad quotes.

quotes.quote: If the answer was an external API, then we would wanna gate the process and have it stop so we could bring in a sales engineer to have a discussion with the customer.
quotes.usage_notes: Concrete example of guided selling logic. Useful for product-led content showing conditional workflows in CPQ.

quotes.quote: Then it goes through implementations quicker because everything is kinda outlined and understood. And if there's complexities, they've already been identified and discussed, and we have the right SKUs on the order.
quotes.usage_notes: Connects quoting quality to implementation speed and time-to-billing. Strong for ROI-oriented content.

quotes.quote: That overall kinda helps with customer satisfaction on the front end and reduces churn on the back end.
quotes.usage_notes: Links quoting process to retention outcome. Useful for executive-level content about quoting as a churn lever.

quotes.quote: There are some things that you guys can do that, if I talk to somebody else, they weren't the same.
quotes.usage_notes: Validates differentiation against unnamed competitors. Useful as a proof point for guided selling and bundle capabilities.

quotes.quote: I like that you guys deal with bundle SKUs and sub SKUs. That's a big plus over what HubSpot does.
quotes.usage_notes: Direct comparison to HubSpot CPQ limitation. Strong for competitive content and HubSpot-vs-Quotivity positioning.

quotes.quote: There are some limitations in HubSpot, and we just gotta see what HubSpot can do. We're gonna go down the internal path first.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the most common competitive dynamic: prospects trying to make HubSpot native work before buying a CPQ. Strong for 'build vs. buy' content.

value_language: capture that earlier in the process
reduces churn on the back end
customer satisfaction on the front end

deviant_terms.meaning: A question-driven quoting flow that asks qualifying questions and routes based on answers. The prospect's primary frame for what they need.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used 5+ times throughout the call. This is the prospect's mental model for the solution.

deviant_terms.meaning: Stop the quoting flow at a specific point to bring in a technical resource (sales engineer) before proceeding. Conditional logic applied to quote progression.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used twice. Represents a specific and concrete workflow requirement.

deviant_terms.meaning: Technical or configuration complexities that arise during quoting and require special handling or escalation. Not objections — more like flags that change the quoting path.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used twice. Internal terminology for configuration triggers.

deviant_terms.meaning: Incorrect technical configurations baked into a quote that cause implementation problems. The downstream cost of ungoverned quoting.
deviant_terms.frequency: Used once but represents a core pain.

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect acknowledged HubSpot limitations around guided selling, bundles, and validation rules. Exploring internal build first.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Where HubSpot native quoting breaks down: guided selling, bundles, conditional logic, and validation rules

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect called out bundle/sub-SKU support as a 'big plus' over HubSpot. Direct gap in native quoting.
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to quote bundled products with sub-SKUs in HubSpot (and why native quoting can't do it)

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect is in telecom SaaS with complex product configurations and integration-dependent quoting paths

keyword_candidates.source: VP of Solutions is sole approval bottleneck, catching errors that should have been prevented earlier

keyword_candidates.source: Prospect needs if-then logic in quoting: if answer is X, add these SKUs; if answer is Y, stop and bring in SE
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Adding conditional logic to HubSpot quotes: rules engines, gating, and auto-population
